topic: SAA western cell drift >=0.3 deg/month — quantitative refinement vs IGRF-13/CHAOS-7 baseline
our_argument_summary: PRED-073 (dome predicts >=0.3 deg SAA western-cell westward drift April->May 2026) is assessed as falsifiable and likely-falsified-by-standard-physics. The analyst refinement (monitor/analyst/expansions/PRED-assessment-PRED-073-refinement.json, integrated by decider 2026-05-24T05:35:52Z) supplies a quantitative IGRF-13/CHAOS-7 baseline: western-cell westward secular variation 0.282 +/- 0.030 deg/year (Finlay et al. 2020), with independent confirmation from CSES proton-flux centroid at 0.33 +/- 0.08 deg/year (Wang et al. 2025). The standard expectation over a 1-month April->May interval is therefore ~0.024 deg, which is below the dome's own published <0.1 deg falsification threshold. Annualised, the dome's >=0.3 deg/month claim is ~3.65 deg/yr, roughly 12-13x the IGRF-13 rate. our_verdict remains pending until CHAOS-7 May 2026 data lands in early June.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Dome makes a single quantitative claim on the predictions page; there is no monitor.py automation for PRED-073. The dome supplies the falsification rule (<0.1 deg westward or reverses direction) and points to CHAOS-7 / NOAA NGDC monthly geomagnetic field maps as the data source. No computation pipeline on the dome side; assessment will be a manual centroid comparison against the May 2026 CHAOS-7 release.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: n/a — prospective prediction, no current observed value to compare. Validation is human-driven on CHAOS-7 release.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: The dome's falsification rule has a gray zone in 0.1-0.3 deg: above the failure threshold but below the confirmation threshold. A measured drift in 0.1-0.2 deg would let the dome claim 'partial confirmation' or 'within noise'. CHAOS-7 centroid spatial uncertainty (~0.1-0.2 deg, Finlay et al. 2020) overlaps this gray zone exactly. This is a real survival corridor.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The analyst's detail_reasoning correctly notes that CHAOS-7 monthly maps for May 2026 should arrive in early June, and that the dome will either post a result or silently ignore.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No domain or function in raw-text/monitor.py touches PRED-073 or SAA western-cell longitude. This is a manually-tracked prediction, not an automated check.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Standard physics (core-mantle differential rotation) already predicts SAA westward secular drift at 0.2-0.5 deg/year. The dome's 'toroidal aetheric return flow' is a relabel of the core-flow mechanism; same direction, similar order-of-magnitude long-term rate. Where the dome diverges (and becomes falsifiable) is in the monthly magnitude — but the underlying phenomenon is standard.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Registered 2026-04-04 for an April->May 2026 test window; data does not exist yet (CHAOS-7 May 2026 release ~early June 2026). This is a genuinely prospective prediction — the dome committed to a magnitude before measurement.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The dome cites mechanism ('toroidal aetheric return flow ... Sump channel') but does not derive the >=0.3 deg/month magnitude from dome geometry (disc_radius=20,015 km, firmament_height=9,086 km, etc.). No formula on the dome site connects geometric parameters to a longitudinal drift rate. The threshold appears stipulated, not derived.

holes_found.description: The detail_reasoning includes gratuitous cognitive-error speculation about the dome author: 'The dome appears to have conflated long-term drift rate (~0.28 deg/year) with short-term excursion (~0.3 deg/month), possibly by treating the annual rate as a monthly rate during prediction authoring.' This is unprovable speculation. The numerical mismatch is the finding; speculating about WHY the dome author chose those numbers (an authoring slip vs a deliberate aether-flux claim) is not the curmudgeon's — or analyst's — place. A dome defender could (and should) push back: the dome explicitly attributes the magnitude to a special mechanism (toroidal aetheric return flow along the Sump channel), and that is the claim under test. Painting it as an arithmetic mistake softens the falsifiability narrative — if it is 'just an authoring slip', then the dome can correct it cheaply and we lose the falsifiability win.
holes_found.recommendation: Strike or rewrite the 'possibly by treating the annual rate as a monthly rate' sentence in detail_reasoning. Keep the numerical comparison and the structural point about the dome's own falsification rule being triggered. Replace the cognitive-error speculation with a neutral observation, e.g., 'whether the magnitude is a deliberate aether-flux claim or an authoring slip is not for us to decide — what matters is that the dome has published the threshold and committed to test it.'

holes_found.description: The sources_cited list includes 'IGRF-13 secular variation tables (https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/IAGA/vmod/igrf13.html)' which returns HTTP 404. NGDC's geomagnetic data products have been migrated under NCEI/NOAA; the canonical landing page is now https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/international-geomagnetic-reference-field (verified HTTP 200), and the BGS mirror at https://geomag.bgs.ac.uk/research/modelling/IGRF.html (also verified 200) is widely used in the literature. A defender pointing at the dead link to suggest sloppy referencing would have a small but real point.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the dead NGDC URL in sources_cited with the NCEI canonical (https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/international-geomagnetic-reference-field) and optionally the BGS mirror. The detail_reasoning itself does not embed the dead URL inline, so no prose change is required — only the sources_cited array.

holes_found.description: The April->May 2026 interval is treated as unambiguously 1 month (30 days) in the annualisation (0.3 deg x 365/30 = 3.65 deg/yr -> 12-13x factor). A defender could read 'April->May 2026' as 'the months of April and May', i.e., a 60-day observation window. Under that reading, the annualised dome rate is 1.825 deg/yr and the factor vs IGRF-13 is ~6.5x, not 12-13x. The falsifiability conclusion is robust either way (standard-physics expectation is ~0.024 deg at 1-month, ~0.047 deg at 2-month, both below the dome's 0.1 deg threshold), but the headline ratio is sensitive. The analyst does not acknowledge the alternative reading.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a one-sentence note in detail_reasoning acknowledging the alternative 2-month reading and stating that the falsification conclusion is robust to both (expected drift 0.024-0.047 deg, dome threshold 0.1 deg). This pre-empts a defender escape via interval re-interpretation.

holes_found.description: The reference 'data/predictions.json passim' in paragraph 3 of detail_reasoning is a vague citation that does not help readers verify the claim ('Compare to most dome predictions, which either restate observations or use threshold values inside the standard-physics confidence band'). NARRATE-CITE discipline (PROP-014 Mech 3) asks for inline citations to specific fields. 'passim' is a Latin equivalent of 'see throughout', which is a non-citation in a quantitative claim.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace with 2-3 specific PRED-IDs that exemplify the pattern (e.g., 'compare PRED-072 — assessed standard_physics, restating a known TTB fluctuation magnitude — and PRED-093 Schumann resonance during G3 storm, which sets a >30% threshold inside the historical SR storm-response band'). Or drop the comparison entirely; the structural argument in the same paragraph (defaults-to-falsified under null hypothesis) is strong enough to stand alone.

holes_found.description: The 0.282 +/- 0.030 deg/year value is attributed to 'Pavon-Carrasco & De Santis, 2016 baseline; Finlay et al. 2020' but the Finlay 2020 paper abstract (verified via CrossRef DOI 10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9) does not quote that specific value — the paper describes CHAOS-7 model construction and qualitative SAA evolution ('westward motion and gathering of reversed flux under South America'), not a numerical western-cell drift rate. The value is plausible and in the literature range, but it is likely a derived figure from CHAOS-7 secular variation tables rather than a direct quote. Defender could request the page/figure citation and find it missing.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) provide a more specific citation — page or figure number from Finlay 2020 if the value is in the paper, or a different paper if it isn't (Pavon-Carrasco & De Santis 2016 or Aubert et al. 2017 are plausible candidates), or (b) re-attribute as 'derived from CHAOS-7 SV coefficients evaluated at the western-cell longitude' which is honest about how the number was obtained. The IGRF-13/CHAOS-7 published consensus on SAA westward drift is 0.25-0.35 deg/yr, so the value is defensible — but the attribution needs tightening.

stronger_arguments.description: The dome's falsifiability commitment here is unusually tight by dome standards: a numerical claim (>=0.3 deg/month) with a published failure threshold (<0.1 deg/month) and a fixed-date observation window. Most dome predictions are either restated observations (post_hoc), directional-only (e.g., 'SAA drifts westward'), or have generous tolerance bands that overlap standard physics. PRED-073's commitment puts it in a small minority of dome predictions that can be cleanly graded against a single CHAOS-7 release. Worth flagging in section text (Part 7 Predictions Tracker) as one of the few prospective dome predictions where standard physics gives a clean default-falsified answer.
sources: data/predictions.json (compare PRED-072: standard_physics, no commitment beyond directional)

stronger_arguments.description: If the dome silent-ignores PRED-073 after CHAOS-7 May data lands, this becomes a publishable case study for the Timestamp Error / Silent Ignore section: dome posted a prediction on 2026-04-04 with a 2026-05-31 close, data lands ~2026-06-05, and (if pattern holds) dome does not post a result. The combination of: (a) dome timestamps observations not predictions, (b) dome's own falsification threshold triggered by the null hypothesis, (c) silent-ignore on standard-physics-default-failures, would be a tight worked example. Worth flagging in Part 6 (Timestamp Error) and Part 7 prediction-tracking section once outcome arrives.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome does correctly identify the SAA western-cell direction of motion (westward) and gets the secular-rate order of magnitude right (0.2-0.5 deg/year is the published consensus, and the dome's aetheric-return-flow mechanism produces values in this band). The mechanism description ('toroidal aetheric return flow carrying bottom-plate flux concentration westward along Sump channel') is a coherent re-framing of the well-known core-mantle differential rotation mechanism that produces westward drift of geomagnetic features — same direction, same order of magnitude, different ontology (data/predictions.json:PRED-073 claim field; monitor/changes/chg-20260523-0703-002.json description field).
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Getting the direction and order-of-magnitude right is the easy part — every standard-physics model of the SAA gets those right too (see Finlay et al. 2020 abstract from CrossRef: 'The continuing growth in size and weakening of the main anomaly is linked to the westward motion and gathering of reversed flux under South America'). What makes PRED-073 distinctively dome-flavoured is the magnitude commitment (>=0.3 deg in ~1 month), and that is precisely where it breaks: the IGRF-13/CHAOS-7 monthly rate is ~0.024 deg, an order of magnitude below the dome's threshold and below the dome's own <0.1 deg failure rule (data/predictions.json:PRED-073 falsification field; sources_cited Finlay 2020 https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9). The kernel-of-truth survival path (mechanism is real, direction is right) does not save the magnitude commitment — and the dome chose to commit to a magnitude precisely so the prediction would be testable, then chose a value that defaults-to-falsified under the consensus null hypothesis.

deeper_analysis: The structural argument (dome's own falsification threshold triggered by the standard-physics null hypothesis) is the load-bearing insight in the analyst refinement, and it is correct. Mathematics are correct under the 1-month reading of April->May; ratios shift to ~6.5x under a 2-month reading but the falsification conclusion is robust either way. Cited sources resolve and are the right papers (Finlay et al. 2020 in EPS 72:156 is the canonical CHAOS-7 SAA paper; Wang et al. 2025 in Science China Earth Sciences is the CSES proton-flux SAA study). The one substantive concern is the gratuitous cognitive-error speculation about the dome author — it softens the falsifiability framing and is not the curmudgeon's call to make. Strip that sentence and the assessment is publishable as-is. Holes (a) dead IGRF-13 URL, (b) interval-reading ambiguity unacknowledged, (c) 'data/predictions.json passim' vagueness, and (d) 0.282 deg/yr attribution looseness are all minor and patchable in a single decider pass. Verdict (pending) is correct — observation does not arrive until early June 2026. Recommend the decider apply a minor-edit patch and re-queue if anything substantive remains.

citations_verified: Finlay, Christopher C., Kloss, Olsen, Hammer, Toffner-Clausen, Grayver, Kuvshinov (2020). 'The CHAOS-7 geomagnetic field model and observed changes in the South Atlantic Anomaly.' Earth, Planets and Space 72:156. DOI 10.1186/s40623-020-01252-9 — resolves via CrossRef; title and authors match analyst citation; abstract confirms paper covers CHAOS-7 construction and SAA westward motion. Specific 0.282 deg/yr value not in abstract — see holes_found.

Wang et al. (2025). 'Investigation of the South Atlantic Anomaly (SAA) by proton flux variabilities based on 6 years CSES data.' Science China Earth Sciences. DOI 10.1007/s11430-025-1672-2 — resolves via CrossRef; matches analyst description; specific 0.33 +/- 0.08 deg/yr value not abstract-verified but plausible.
Pavon-Carrasco & De Santis (2016) baseline reference — implied but not directly cited with DOI/URL by analyst; standard SAA literature reference, plausible.
citations_failed: https://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/IAGA/vmod/igrf13.html — HTTP 404. NGDC migrated geomagnetic products to NCEI; canonical landing page now https://www.ncei.noaa.gov/products/international-geomagnetic-reference-field (verified HTTP 200). BGS mirror https://geomag.bgs.ac.uk/research/modelling/IGRF.html also verified 200.
citations_unchecked: Cross-reference 'data/predictions.json passim' — not a specific citation; not auditable as written.

Specific value 0.282 +/- 0.030 deg/yr for western-cell westward drift — paper resolves but value is not in the publicly-fetchable Finlay 2020 abstract; would require paper-body access to fully verify. Range (0.2-0.4 deg/yr) is consistent with published literature.

summary_for_decider: Verification passes. The PRED-073 refinement is sound: math correct, sources real, falsifiability argument structurally robust, our_verdict (pending) appropriate. Five findings — one moderate (drop cognitive-error speculation about dome author in detail_reasoning), four minor (fix dead IGRF-13 URL in sources_cited, acknowledge April->May 1-vs-2-month interval ambiguity, replace 'passim' citation with specific PRED-IDs, tighten 0.282 deg/yr attribution). All are single-pass patchable. No verdict change needed. Decider can either self-apply patches or defer until CHAOS-7 May 2026 data arrives in early June (when a verdict update will happen anyway). Carry-over: none — this is cycle 1, no prior review existed.
